title: API Routes
description: Type-safe API routes with Zod validation, hooks and OpenAPI generation

blocks.text: API routes are apis/**/*.api.ts files, each default-exporting a defineApi definition. Use the pre-typed defineApi from howl.config.ts (State + roles already bound). Enable with app.fsApiRoutes(apiConfig).

rows: Human-readable name — used in OpenAPI docs and error logs.
GET, POST, PUT, PATCH or DELETE.
Map of status code → Zod schema describing the body.
The request handler. Return value is the response body (see below).
Explicit path override (string or string[]). Otherwise inferred from the file path.
Zod schema for the JSON body — validated and typed onto ctx.req.body.
Zod schema for query params — validated and typed onto ctx.query().
Per-route RateLimitConfig, or false to disable. Overrides defaultRateLimit.

blocks.text: The file's location is authoritative: the path is inferred from where the file sits under apis/, prefixed with /api. Use [param] folders for path params. Set an explicit path to override.

apis/public/ping.api.ts            →  GET  /api/public/ping
apis/users/create.api.ts           →  POST /api/users/create
apis/users/[id]/posts.api.ts       →  GET  /api/users/:id/posts
// path: "/webhooks/stripe"  →  overrides inference entirely

Two routes can share a URL slot under different param names — GET /api/task/:id and POST /api/task/:projectId — and each resolves to its own handler by method; a request never 405s just because another param name was registered first. A genuine method miss returns 405 with an Allow header, and OPTIONS returns 204 with the same; both list the union of verbs across every pattern overlapping the slot.

Howl validates path params, query, and the JSON body against their Zod schemas before the handler runs. Typed values arrive on ctx.req.body and ctx.query(); a validation failure returns 400 before your handler is called.
Body validation only runs for JSON. multipart/form-data and x-www-form-urlencoded streams are left untouched, so a handler can call ctx.req.formData() safely for file uploads.

A handler's return value is the body. Howl lifts statusCode (or status) out as the HTTP status, strips ok, and emits the rest with ok: true injected. It does not auto-nest a data field — return one explicitly if you want it.
You can also return a raw Response from a handler when you need full control (custom headers, a redirect, a stream).

Throw an HttpError for a controlled status. Unexpected throws return a generic message — only HttpError (and sub-500 statuses) expose their message. The real error and stack are logged under a correlation id, returned in the X-Howl-Correlation-Id header.

Hooks run around the handler. before runs post-auth/validation; returning a Response short-circuits it. after runs on every successful response (including cache hits) and may replace it. Both get the app instance — for jobs, auditing, metrics.
An OpenAPI 3.1 spec is generated from your schemas at crawl time and held in a singleton. Expose it on any route, behind any guard — Howl does not auto-mount it.
The same schemas also drive the typed HTTP client the build generates from your apis/ directory — see the Plugins page.
